name: Hispanic Vulgar Latin

ancestors.notes: Celtic substrate (Gallaecian / Celtiberian). Likely contributed to retention of /w/, initial f- weakening, and prosodic tendencies.
Aquitanian / proto-Basque substrate: probable source of f- → h- weakening.
Lusitanian substrate: possible influence on preservation of /w/ and /v/~/b/ distinction.
Iberian substrate: language isolate of Mediterranean Iberia. ~2000+ inscriptions in Iberian semi-syllabary and Greek alphabet.

notes: Hispanic Vulgar Latin (~3rd–7th c.). Transitional spoken Latin in Roman Hispania. Phonological innovations: (1) Classical vowel-length distinction lost; 10-vowel → 7-vowel merger by quality. (2) Lenition: intervocalic P/T/C → B/D/G → β/ð/ɣ. (3) Early palatalisation: /k/→[tʃ] before e/i; /g/→[dʒ] before e/i. (4) Initial /f/ weakening to [h] in much of Iberia. (5) /s/ → [z] intervocalically. (6) Nasal assimilation producing [ŋ] in coda. (7) Diphthong developments setting up later Romance outcomes. This is the immediate ancestor of all Ibero-Romance languages, including Galaico-Portuguese.

sources.author: Lloyd, P.M.
sources.title: From Latin to Spanish, vol. I

sources.author: Weiss, M.
sources.title: Outline of the Historical and Comparative Grammar of Latin
